The city settled that so far as we

ordinance" declared President
Howard Elliot of the Northern Pa
cific today.

one suits us we'll enough. Only lit
tie towns speak of those things.
Spokane is too large town to talk

Great Northern.


Those assertions, coming FROM
the two greatest railroad builders
in the country, seem to settle fOn
the present at least two Of the


railway problems which confront
this city.


The distinguished party Of raiE


way officials, including James u

Mutt, vice president of the Spo-
kane, Portland & Seattle, arrived

pire system and taken to Potlatch,
Idaho, where this morning the

route back to Spokane, where they

They were met in automobiles
by committee comprising

J. d. Porter, L. Mclean and w.
D. Nichols, and escorted to the

The address of welcome was de.
livered by President F. e. Goodall


of the chamber of commerce; Rev.


w. ]. Hindley followed with q
speech ON Inland Empire progress.
James j. Hill responded.
Mr.. Hill, previous to the banquet,


was reticent. apparently, to dis


cuss general topics. He said, how
| ever, that his line has no concern


in the subject of union depot. He


said that only little towns have


union depots, inferring that in


large towns railway companies


have sufficient depot accommodar
| tons.


I'm much pleased at the way
the election went" he said.
Howard Elliot, president OF the


Northern Pacific, when asked as to


the prospects of grade separation


unhesitatingly stated that so far
as the railway was concerned IL
| was a dead issue.


THE city settled that matter for
u.s." he said, "and we do not intend


to reopen the matter"
Referring to the election, he said


he believed that Taft's success as
sures good times.


In the Hall of the Doges large


crowd of representative citizens


were seated awaiting the arrival OF


the railroad men. On the appear


ance of Mr.. Hill he was instantly
recognized and the gathering arose.
